steps.title: Identify the appositive
steps.content: ”Costa Rica” clarifies which “fellow OECD nations” are being referenced. This is an appositive — a noun phrase that specifies or renames a general category.

steps.title: Apply appositive punctuation
steps.content: Standard English requires a comma to mark the boundary between the general noun “nations” and its appositive “Costa Rica,” giving us “fellow OECD nations, Costa Rica and Switzerland.” The other choices either omit the comma, use a colon to introduce a list, or use a semicolon for independent clauses—none of which correctly punctuate an appositive. That matches choice C — C is correct.
